profile.summary: A Senior Nurse usually delivers complex patient care while coordinating day-to-day clinical work on a ward, unit, or service. They often guide junior nurses, support handovers, manage patient flow, escalate deterioration, and work closely with doctors, allied health staff, patients, and families. Scope varies by setting, but the role commonly blends hands-on care, clinical judgment, and informal team leadership.

known_challenges.text: Balancing direct patient care with supervising junior staff and keeping the shift running smoothly.
High emotional load from patient deterioration, distressed families, and repeated exposure to illness or death.
Maintaining safe care when staffing is short, acuity is high, or admissions and discharges are moving quickly.
Giving clear escalation and clinical direction without always having formal authority over everyone on shift.
Keeping documentation, medication safety, and handovers accurate under constant interruption.

recommended_question_themes.why: This role often absorbs pressure when patient needs rise or staffing gaps appear, so sustainability and safety matter.
Senior nurses are expected to spot deterioration early and act decisively, so confidence and backup are important.

recommended_question_themes.theme: Supporting junior nurses and healthcare assistants
recommended_question_themes.why: They often carry informal leadership and teaching work that affects team confidence and patient care.
Small process failures in these areas can quickly become patient safety risks in a busy clinical setting.
Repeated exposure to distress, conflict, and loss can build up even when performance looks steady.

terminology.meaning: How unwell and resource-intensive a patient is.
Record of vital signs and trends over time.
Documented plan for treatment, nursing care, and goals.
Structured format for urgent clinical communication.
Transfer of patient information and responsibility between staff.
Raising concerns to get timely senior or urgent support.
Preparing safe transition from hospital or service.
Formal record of a safety event or near miss.
Structured support for a newly qualified nurse.
Assigning tasks while keeping accountability for safe care.
Using assessment and experience to decide care.
Movement of patients through beds, wards, or services.
Coordinating bed use to match patient needs.

listen_for: Whether they talk mostly about patient safety risks or only about getting through the shift.
How they describe escalation: early and clear, or delayed because support feels unavailable.
Whether teaching and supporting junior staff sounds rewarding, draining, or invisible.
Repeated mention of missed breaks, staying late, or carrying extra patients.
Shifts in language from confident clinical judgment to second-guessing after difficult events.
avoid: Treating them as only a bedside clinician and missing the coordination and leadership load they carry.
Assuming resilience means they are coping well with grief, conflict, or chronic pressure.
Pushing speed or throughput without acknowledging patient safety and documentation risks.
Undermining their judgment by framing escalations as overreactions rather than safety decisions.
